query: definition of poliosis circumscripta in dermatology

retrieved_docs.title: Lichenification -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Basic skin lesions divide into primary, secondary, and special types. The term lichenification is classed as a secondary skin lesion wherein the characteristic features of skin thickening, hyperpigmentation, and exaggerated skin lines are noted. Lichenification can be further divided into primary and secondary types wherein primary lichenification signifies lichen simplex chronicus, also known as neurodermatitis circumscripta. On the other hand, secondary lichenification occurs in atopic dermatitis, infective eczematous dermatoses, psoriasis, psoriasiform dermatosis, xerosis, pityriasis rubra pilaris, porokeratosis, vegetative growths, anxiety, and obsessive-compulsive disorders. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypopigmented Macules -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Definitions of Macroscopic Terms
retrieved_docs.content: Macule - Flattened skin lesion less than 5mm in size, distinguished by discoloration compared to surrounding normal skin Patch - Macular skin lesion with more than 5mm in size Lichenification - Thickened and rough skin with prominent skin markings, often due to repeated rubbing

retrieved_docs.title: Koebner Phenomenon -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The Koebner phenomenon requires differentiation from the reverse Koebner phenomenon, which is the disappearance of skin lesions following trauma over the existing lesions. [3] When working on a differential diagnosis, it is also important to distinguish the Koebner phenomenon from the pseudo-Koebner phenomenon (also known as category II Koebner phenomenon as per the Boyd and Nelder classification), in which infectious agents (such as viruses) cause the spread of monomorphic lesions across previously unaffected skin (see Table. Classification of Koebner and Koebner-Related Responses). [4] The pseudo-Koebner phenomenon occurs with common infectious etiologies, such as molluscum contagiosum and verrucae [infective warts caused by human papillomavirus]. [5] See Image. Pseudo-Koebnerization.

retrieved_docs.title: Koplik Spots -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Koplik spots are the peculiar spots present on the buccal mucosa and are considered a diagnostic/pathognomic feature of measles/rubeola in the pre-eruptive stage. The term Koplik spot derives its name from Dr. Henry Koplik of New York, who first described them in 1896.

retrieved_docs.title: Porokeratosis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to clinical evaluation, the best method for definitive diagnosis is a biopsy of the elevated border. Biopsy in this area will show the cornoid lamella and will be diagnostic. Clinically, porokeratosis can resemble other skin conditions, such as psoriasis, lichen simplex chronicus, hypertrophic lichen planus, tuberculosis of the skin, Bowen disease, candidiasis, irritants, and allergic contact dermatitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Koebner Phenomenon -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: One should also differentiate the true Koebner phenomenon from Wolf’s isotopic response (see Image. True Koebner Phenomenon). Wolf’s isotopic response is similar to when trauma induces new skin lesions to appear on previously unaffected skin. However, unlike the Koebner phenomenon, the lesions in Wolf’s precede the appearance of generalized skin disease. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypopigmented Macules -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Definitions of Microscopic Terms
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkeratosis - Thickened Stratum corneum, often associated with qualitative keratin abnormality Parakeratosis - Keratinization with retained nuclei of Stratum corneum; keratinization on mucous membranes is considered normal Spongiosis - Intercellular edema of the epidermis Acanthosis - An overgrowth of stratum spinosum Hydropic swelling - Intracellular edema of keratinocytes, often seen in viral infections Exocytosis - Infiltration of the epidermis by inflammatory cells Lentiginous - A linear pattern of melanocyte proliferation within the epidermal basal cell layer Epidermotropism - Migration of lymphocytes into epidermis without accompanying spongiosis

retrieved_docs.title: Lichenification -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Diffuse types of lichenification have been described wherein involvement of the face, neck, chest, abdomen, flanks, arms, forearms, thighs and lower legs appear as ill-defined plaques ranging from hyperpigmented patches, numerous flat-topped papules, and indurated plaques may present symmetrically with extremity involvement. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act -- Definition/Introduction -- 1983 Orphan Drug Act (PL 97-414)
retrieved_docs.content: This Act incentivizes the development of “orphan drugs” to treat rare diseases such as cystic fibrosis, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and Huntington disease. Incentives include tax benefits, expedited regulatory reviews, clinical research subsidies, and extended market exclusivity. Before the Orphan Drug Act passed, only ten orphan drugs were available for patient use. As of 2015, the FDA has approved over 550 orphan drugs to treat 277 rare diseases. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Atrophoderma of Pasini and Pierini -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: A neurogenic cause has been suggested because of the apparent zosteriform distribution of lesions in some cases, but definitive evidence is lacking. [2] There is growing evidence to consider the atrophoderma of Pasini and Pierini as a manifestation of the spectrum of localized scleroderma. [3] , [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypomelanosis of Ito -- Evaluation -- Minor criteria:
retrieved_docs.content: Documented chromosomal anomalies indicative of mosaicism Two or more congenital anomalies not involving the nervous or musculoskeletal systems The diagnosis is supported by the presence of both major criteria, or 1 major and 1 minor, or 2 minor criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: Lichenification -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Owing to the inciting factor of chronic rubbing, lichenification often occurs in easily accessible sites such as the nape of the neck, wrists, hands, forearms, waist, scrotum, vulva, thighs, lower legs and dorsa of feet. [6] Certain cases are known to occur in the lower eyelids, postauricular area, axillae, and popliteal fossae. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Lichenification -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The lesions start as hyperpigmentation on a flat skin surface that is followed by the appearance of multiple small papules, termed pseudopapules, giving the lesion a pebbly appearance. The next is thickening or induration of the deep layers of the skin that does not involve the skin creases and thereby imparts the rather characteristic exaggeration of skin markings. Lesions appear as ill-defined except for certain cases of secondary lichenification. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Osler Node and Janeway Lesions -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Osler nodes and Janeway lesions are typically differentiated based on their morphology, location, distribution, histology, and, importantly, clinical context. The differential diagnosis includes palpable purpura as found in various types of small and medium vessel vasculitis, including microscopic polyarteritis, granulomatosis with polyangiitis, eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis, immunoglobulin A vasculitis, cutaneous small-vessel vasculitis, leukocytoclastic vasculitis, and polyarteritis nodosa. Palpable purpura may also be a finding in paraneoplastic or malignancy-associated-vasculitis, disseminated gonococcal disease, disseminated intravascular coagulation, meningococcemia, Rocky Mountain spotted fever, and Buerger disease. Other causes of purple macules and patches include drug eruptions (glucocorticoid-induced purpura), arthropod bites, sun exposure (actinic/senile purpura), and rarely calciphylaxis. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Atrophoderma of Pasini and Pierini -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Atrophoderma of Pasini and Pierini is a rare disease that most likely will not be encountered by the primary care provider or nurse practitioner. However, when faced with unknown skin lesions, the best recommendation is to refer the patient to a dermatologist for a definitive diagnosis and then work as a team to provide coordinated follow up. Atrophoderma of Pasini and Pierini has a marked female preponderance, with a female to male patient ratio of 6:1. The condition is more frequently reported in white Europeans. The disorder may progress in some people, but little is known about its long term course.

retrieved_docs.title: Laser Fitzpatrick Skin Type Recommendations -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: By nature of the criteria, Fitzpatrick's skin type classifications are subjective; however, use of the scale has proven valuable as a diagnostic and therapeutic evaluation tool for many situations. Specifically, these classifications are commonly employed to estimate dosages of particular ultraviolet B (UVB), psoralen with ultraviolet A (PUVA), and laser treatments, as well as to predict skin cancer risk and guide sun protection advice. [3] Though widely used throughout dermatology, plastic surgery, surgical oncology, and other specialties, the Fitzpatrick skin types are not without controversy. Current questions regarding the objective determination, accuracy of self-reporting, and effectiveness in ethnic skin are all major issues of concern with Fitzpatrick skin types. See Images. Fitzpatrick Skin Type Quiz, Questionnaire Pages 1 and 2.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2588
retrieved_docs.content: NICHD Terminology is a classification framework that incorporates a range of health‑related concepts. Within its scope it includes the concept of vakcíny proti chřipce (influenza vaccines) and the concept of التهاب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِي جرثومي (bacterial intestinal inflammatory disease). The terminology also contains the concepts الهَلْوَسة and حالة ارتباكية as members of its subset. Additionally, the concept حُمامَى (a skin condition such as pimples or acne) is included in the NICHD Terminology subset, and حُمامَى is noted to be associated with other inflammatory conditions of the skin. This interconnected grouping highlights how the terminology links preventive measures, gastrointestinal inflammation, neurological confusion states, dermatologic manifestations, and their broader inflammatory relationships.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2178
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “سرطان الجلد الخبيث” (malignant skin cancer) is mapped from the broader description “ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى” (malignant secondary tumor in other specific locations), indicating that malignant skin cancer is understood as a form of malignant secondary tumor that can arise in particular anatomical sites. This relationship clarifies that the skin manifestation of a malignant secondary tumor is classified under the specific term “سرطان الجلد الخبيث,” linking the general category of secondary malignant neoplasms to its specific cutaneous presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2356
retrieved_docs.content: Other non‑epithelial cancer of skin is a broader classification that encompasses the condition described in Arabic as ورم خبيث في الجلد، آخر, indicating an additional malignant tumor of the skin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1251
retrieved_docs.content: The NICHD Terminology encompasses a diverse subset of medical concepts, including the Czech term “vakcíny proti chřipce” (influenza vaccines) and the Arabic term “التهاب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِي جرثومي” (bacterial gastroenteritis). Within the same subset are the Arabic concepts “الهَلْوَسة” and “حالة ارتباكية,” which refer to a specific condition and a confusional state, respectively. The terminology also includes the Arabic terms “حُمامَى” and “داء المتشاخسات.” The condition “داء المتشاخسات” is mapped from the concept of noninfectious gastroenteritis, indicating that it represents a form of gastroenteritis not caused by infection. Additionally, the skin condition described as “other inflammatory condition of skin” is associated with “حُمامَى,” suggesting a clinical link between this inflammatory skin disorder and the entity “حُمامَى.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, a complication can be associated with the presence of multiple open wounds that themselves are not complicated, indicating that a broader clinical complication may arise even when each individual wound does not exhibit additional problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 812
retrieved_docs.content: Burns, referred to in Arabic as حرق حراري, denote thermal injuries to the skin and underlying tissues; the term حرق حراري directly describes the heat‑induced nature of these lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 473
retrieved_docs.content: within the terminology. A mapping relationship links “noninfectious gastroenteritis” to “داء المتشاخسات,” suggesting that the latter is considered a specific manifestation or synonym of the former within the classification system. Finally, an “other inflammatory condition of skin” is associated with “حُمامَى,” indicating that this term represents a particular skin inflammation within the broader category of dermatologic inflammatory disorders. Collectively, these interconnections illustrate how the NICHD Terminology organizes related diseases, how specific clinical conditions (such as short stature) are tied to perinatal risk factors, and how hierarchical and associative relationships clarify the taxonomy of infectious, inflammatory, and vascular disorders for improved understanding and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: The term Anden general/uspec sygdom, which denotes an other general or unspecified disease, is linked across languages to the Slovak word nemoc meaning disease and also to the Arabic term الختان referring to circumcision. In this framework, the concept nemoc is further associated with اضطراب استقلاب الأحماض الأمينية, an amino‑acid‑metabolism disorder. This illustrates how a broad disease category can correspond to both a generic disease label and a specific medical procedure, and that diseases in general are related to metabolic disturbances such as amino‑acid metabolism disorders, highlighting the importance of precise terminology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2205
retrieved_docs.content: The Danish classification “Anden general/uspec sygdom” is mapped to the Arabic term “الختان”, which denotes circumcision, indicating that this category of other general or unspecified disease corresponds to the circumcision procedure in Arabic medical terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2838
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a classification that designates permissible modifiers in medical terminology. Within this framework, the descriptors “calculus of urinary tract” and “diseases of white blood cells” are allowed qualifiers for the term moč, indicating that these conditions may be used to modify or specify findings related to moč. Conversely, the term moč itself is an allowed qualifier for the Arabic concepts البلوغ (puberty) and سعفة الرأس (tinea capitis), showing that moč can be employed to modify or describe these clinical entities. This bidirectional qualifying system highlights how specific medical concepts can serve both as targets for qualification and as qualifiers for other conditions, facilitating precise and consistent communication across different languages and areas of patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose veins of the lower extremity (تدلي الأوردة في الاطراف السفلية) are identified as a parent category for a generic Diagnosis/Diseases Component and are also listed as a concept within the NICHD Terminology. Morphologically, varicose veins of the lower extremity are associated with ulceration (قَرحَة). The NICHD Terminology organizes a broad range of pediatric and perinatal concepts: tube feeding (التغذية بالأنبوب), a state of confusion (حالة ارتباكية), short stature (قصر القامة), and the condition of “halwa” (الهَلْوَسة) are each designated as concepts in specific subsets, while the subsets also include influenza vaccines (vakcíny proti chřipce), bacterial gastroenteritis (التهاب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِي جرثومي), varicose veins of the lower extremity, threat of preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر), skin eruptions such as pimples (حُمامَى), and the disease known as “dysentery” (داء المتشاخسات). Clinically, infant preterm low birth weight is associated with short stature, and a threat of preterm labor is likewise linked to short stature, underscoring the impact of early‑life growth restriction on later height outcomes. The condition “dysentery” (داء المتشاخسات) is mapped from non‑infectious gastroenteritis, indicating a conceptual overlap between these gastrointestinal disorders. Additionally, other inflammatory skin conditions are associated with pimples (حُمامَى), reflecting a relationship between cutaneous inflammation and common dermatologic manifestations. Together, these interconnections illustrate how vascular abnormalities, growth‑related outcomes, infectious and inflammatory conditions, and specific therapeutic or preventive measures are organized within a comprehensive pediatric terminology framework, informing both clinical assessment and patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary congenital anomalies and sexually transmitted infections are both listed as members of the Arabic‑named SMQ “الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ)”, which groups together congenital, familial and genetic disorders. This classification indicates that, within this standardized medical terminology, these two distinct clinical categories are considered part of a broader collection of disorders that share underlying developmental, hereditary, or genetic characteristics. Recognizing their inclusion in the same SMQ can aid clinicians and researchers in organizing diagnostic codes, facilitating epidemiological studies, and ensuring comprehensive patient care that addresses both congenital urogenital conditions and infections transmitted through sexual contact under a unified framework.
